entries.title: Yogurt is runny or watery
entries.question: why is my yogurt runny
entries.answer: Runny yogurt is usually caused by low-fat milk, short fermentation time, weak starter culture, or disturbance during fermentation. Use whole milk and extend fermentation to 10–12 hours.

entries.title: Yogurt did not set
entries.question: yogurt didn't set
entries.answer: This may happen if the starter culture is inactive, the milk temperature is unsuitable, or the fermentation time is too short. Use fresh starter and keep the lid closed during fermentation.

entries.title: Yogurt too sour
entries.question: why is yogurt too sour
entries.answer: Over-fermentation causes sour taste. Reduce fermentation time to about 6–8 hours if you prefer a milder flavor.

entries.title: Yogurt separated
entries.question: why yogurt separated
entries.answer: Separation or whey on top is normal. You can stir it back in or strain it to make thicker Greek-style yogurt.

entries.title: Fermentation time
entries.question: how long to ferment yogurt
entries.answer: The default yogurt program is about 8 hours. For thicker yogurt, extend fermentation to 10–12 hours. The machine supports longer fermentation up to 24 hours.

entries.title: Fermentation temperature
entries.question: what temperature yogurt maker
entries.answer: The yogurt maker maintains a suitable fermentation temperature, around 104°F or 40°C for yogurt mode.

entries.title: Best milk type
entries.question: what milk for yogurt
entries.answer: Whole milk gives thicker and creamier yogurt. Low-fat or skim milk usually produces thinner yogurt.

entries.title: Starter culture amount
entries.question: how much starter yogurt
entries.answer: Use about 5g of starter culture, or about 4 tablespoons of plain yogurt as starter, following the manual guidance.

entries.title: Preheating milk
entries.question: do I need to heat milk
entries.answer: If using pasteurized milk, preheating is optional. For best results, keep the milk at or below about 104°F or 40°C before adding starter.

entries.title: Make Greek yogurt
entries.question: how to make greek yogurt
entries.answer: Use the Greek yogurt function if available, then strain the yogurt to remove whey for a thicker texture.

entries.title: General plant milk use
entries.question: can I use plant milk for yogurt
entries.answer: Plant-based milk can be used, but results depend heavily on protein content, starter culture, and recipe. Soy milk usually works best among plant milks.

entries.title: Opening lid during fermentation
entries.question: can I open yogurt maker
entries.answer: Do not open the lid during fermentation unless necessary, because it may disturb temperature stability and affect the result.

entries.title: Machine not heating
entries.question: yogurt maker not heating
entries.answer: Check the power connection and make sure the machine is properly started. If the problem continues, contact support.

entries.title: Display not working
entries.question: display not working
entries.answer: Check whether the power cord is connected securely and try another outlet. If the display still does not light up, contact support.

entries.title: How to clean
entries.question: how to clean yogurt maker
entries.answer: Wash the jars and accessories with warm soapy water. Do not immerse the machine base in water.

entries.title: How to store yogurt
entries.question: how to store yogurt
entries.answer: Store finished yogurt in the refrigerator, ideally for up to 5–7 days depending on freshness and ingredients.

entries.title: Best fermentation time
entries.question: best time yogurt
entries.answer: About 8 hours is typical for regular yogurt. For thicker yogurt, try 10–12 hours.

entries.title: Yogurt not thick enough
entries.question: yogurt not thick enough
entries.answer: Use whole milk, make sure your starter is active, and extend fermentation time to 10–12 hours.

entries.title: Overheating concern
entries.question: is yogurt maker too hot
entries.answer: The machine is designed to maintain a safe fermentation temperature for yogurt. Slight warmth is normal.

entries.title: Batch size
entries.question: how much yogurt can I make
entries.answer: You can make a batch according to the included containers and machine capacity. Follow the fill guidance in the manual and do not overfill.

entries.title: Reuse yogurt as starter
entries.question: can I reuse yogurt
entries.answer: Yes, you can reuse homemade yogurt as starter for a few batches, but the culture may weaken over time.

entries.title: Add sugar
entries.question: can I add sugar
entries.answer: For the most reliable fermentation, add sugar after fermentation is complete unless your recipe specifically requires otherwise.

entries.title: Add fruit
entries.question: can I add fruit
entries.answer: It is usually better to add fruit after fermentation. Adding fruit first may affect the fermentation environment.

entries.title: Noise level
entries.question: is yogurt maker noisy
entries.answer: The yogurt maker is generally quiet during operation because fermentation does not require a motor like a blender does.

entries.title: Power usage
entries.question: power consumption
entries.answer: The yogurt maker is a low-power appliance designed for gentle fermentation over time.

entries.title: Overnight use
entries.question: can I leave overnight
entries.answer: Yes, overnight fermentation is normal for yogurt making, as long as the machine is used according to instructions.

entries.title: Container choice
entries.question: can I use other jars
entries.answer: Use heat-safe containers that fit properly inside the machine and do not interfere with the lid closing.

entries.title: Probiotics
entries.question: is yogurt healthy
entries.answer: Yogurt is commonly valued for probiotics and can be part of a balanced diet, depending on ingredients and personal needs.

entries.title: Fermentation failed
entries.question: why fermentation failed
entries.answer: Fermentation may fail because of weak starter, unsuitable milk temperature, low protein ingredients, or insufficient fermentation time.

entries.title: Room temperature effect
entries.question: room temperature affect yogurt
entries.answer: The machine controls the fermentation environment, so room temperature usually has limited impact unless the surrounding conditions are extreme.

entries.title: Can I make plant-based yogurt
entries.question: can I make plant yogurt
entries.answer: Yes, but plant-based milk behaves differently from dairy milk. The yogurt maker mainly provides temperature, so results depend on protein content, culture, and recipe.

entries.title: Soy milk yogurt
entries.question: can I use soy milk for yogurt
entries.answer: Yes, soy milk usually works best among plant milks because it has relatively higher protein. Use unsweetened soy milk and a suitable starter culture.

entries.title: Almond milk yogurt too thin
entries.question: why almond milk yogurt is watery
entries.answer: Almond milk is usually low in protein, so it often results in thin or watery yogurt. You may need a suitable starter and a thickener for better texture.

entries.title: Coconut milk not setting
entries.question: coconut milk yogurt not setting
entries.answer: Coconut milk may not set well on its own because structure depends on recipe and stabilizing ingredients. Full-fat coconut milk and thickener may help.

entries.title: Oat milk yogurt issue
entries.question: oat milk yogurt not working
entries.answer: Oat milk is often difficult to turn into thick yogurt because it is relatively low in protein. It may require recipe adjustments and thickening support.

entries.title: Protein requirement
entries.question: why plant yogurt not thick
entries.answer: Plant milk often lacks enough protein to build a stable yogurt texture. That is why plant yogurt may stay thin or loose.

entries.title: Need proper culture
entries.question: what starter for plant yogurt
entries.answer: Use a starter culture suitable for plant-based yogurt when possible. Regular dairy yogurt starter may not always work reliably with plant milk.

entries.title: Need thickener
entries.question: how to thicken plant yogurt
entries.answer: Plant yogurt often needs thickeners such as starch, pectin, agar, or similar stabilizers to achieve a more spoonable texture.

entries.title: Result is still liquid
entries.question: plant yogurt still liquid
entries.answer: If the result stays liquid, the likely causes are low protein content, unsuitable starter, or a recipe that needs thickening support.

entries.title: Plant yogurt separation
entries.question: plant yogurt separated
entries.answer: Separation is common in plant yogurt because plant ingredients usually do not form the same stable protein network as dairy milk.

entries.title: Greek yogurt with plant milk
entries.question: can I make greek yogurt with almond milk
entries.answer: Greek-style plant yogurt is more difficult because plant milk does not behave like dairy milk during straining. Recipe design matters a lot.

entries.title: Lactose-free and plant milk mix
entries.question: can I mix lactose free and almond milk
entries.answer: Mixing can be done, but fermentation quality depends on the overall protein and sugar balance of the mixture.

entries.title: Longer fermentation needed
entries.question: plant yogurt fermentation time
entries.answer: Plant yogurt may need a longer fermentation time than regular dairy yogurt, sometimes around 12–16 hours depending on the recipe.

entries.title: Recipe dependency
entries.question: why plant yogurt inconsistent
entries.answer: Plant yogurt results depend heavily on the specific recipe, ingredients, and starter. The machine mainly provides temperature support.

entries.title: Machine limitation
entries.question: does yogurt maker work for plant milk
entries.answer: The yogurt maker provides the fermentation environment, but success with plant milk depends mainly on ingredients, starter culture, and formulation.
keywords: does yogurt maker work for plant milk
